title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
**Review of "Bayesian Functional Mixed Model for Time-Series Data with Norm-Preserving Warping"**

**Overall Assessment:**

This paper introduces a novel Bayesian functional mixed model designed for estimating fixed effect functions in time-series data. It uniquely accounts for population- and object-level variability, measurement error, and a confounding norm-preserving warping function. The model's utility is demonstrated through both simulations and real-world functional data examples. Despite some areas for improvement in its comparative evaluation, the paper makes a significant contribution to the field of functional data analysis, particularly with its clear exposition and robust methodology for handling norm-preserving warps. The work is well-motivated and implemented, positioning it to advance the application of these methods. I recommend acceptance.

**Strengths:**

The paper offers a commendable introduction to various temporal warping models, providing excellent intuition and supporting it with numerous examples. A particular strength lies in the illuminating mathematical and visual explanations differentiating between value-preserving and norm-preserving warps, which is crucial for understanding the model's distinct contribution. The work is well-positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis, especially given the growing applications of these sophisticated methods. Furthermore, the authors effectively motivate their proposed mixed effects models by referencing historical examples from 2D shape analysis, providing valuable context. The strategies employed for implementing infinite-dimensional computations through projections onto basis functions, such as B-splines, are effective and clearly explained.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**

While the paper presents a strong methodological contribution, there are several areas where the empirical evaluation could be significantly strengthened.

1.  **Limited and Potentially Unfair Comparisons:** The current comparisons are limited to a single state-of-the-art technique, 'warpMix', utilizing its default R implementation parameters. This raises concerns about fairness, as the default noise variance in 'warpMix' (around 10^-3) is significantly different from the proposed model's noise variances (e.g., $\sigma_c^2 = 0.25$, $\sigma^2 = 1$).
    *   **Suggestion:** For a more equitable comparison, I strongly recommend attempting to match the noise variance parameters between the 'warpMix' model and the proposed model. This would allow for a clearer assessment of the proposed method's performance under similar noise conditions.

2.  **Scope of Comparative Literature:** The paper currently does not compare the proposed approach to other relevant literature, particularly papers by cited authors (e.g., Kurtek, Srivastava, and colleagues) that utilize similar norm-preserving action models. Given the novelty of the norm-preserving aspect, such comparisons would greatly enhance the paper's contribution and contextualize its performance.
    *   **Suggestion:** Please include comparisons with other existing approaches from the literature that employ similar norm-preserving action models.

3.  **Subjective Evaluation of Real Data:** The evaluation for real functional data examples relies heavily on subjective visual assessments of quality. It is unclear if the estimated functions are useful in a quantifiable way, as there is no demonstration of improvement in downstream tasks like classification.
    *   **Suggestion:** Augment the evaluation of real functional data with objective, quantifiable metrics derived from downstream tasks (e.g., classification accuracy, prediction error) to robustly demonstrate the practical utility of the estimated functions.

4.  **Implications of Basis Function Approximation:** The paper effectively uses basis functions (e.g., B-splines) for practical implementation. However, a deeper discussion on the implications of this approximation would be beneficial.
    *   **Suggestion:** Investigate and discuss the implications of implementing the method without basis function approximations (i.e., using raw samples), detailing what aspects would simplify or complicate the process, and the practical consequences of assuming specific families of basis functions like B-splines. This could also touch upon the findings from the appendix regarding data-driven functional PCA bases.

**Conclusion:**

This paper introduces a highly relevant and well-articulated Bayesian functional mixed model for time-series data, notably incorporating norm-preserving warping. The clarity and depth in explaining the theoretical underpinnings and practical implementation via basis functions are significant strengths. While the current empirical evaluation could be strengthened by more comprehensive and fairer comparisons, and by incorporating quantifiable metrics for real-world data, these are addressable points. The identified weaknesses do not detract from the core methodological contribution and the overall quality of the work. I believe this paper represents a valuable addition to the functional data analysis literature and recommend its acceptance.

The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

7

extracted_ideas.summary: The paper presents a Bayesian functional mixed model designed to estimate fixed effect functions in time-series data, accounting for population- and object-level variability, measurement error, and a confounding norm-preserving warping function. It employs specific distributional assumptions and basis function approximations, demonstrating its utility through simulations and real-world functional data examples.
strengths: Provides a good introduction to various temporal warping models and their underlying intuition, supported by many examples.
Offers illuminating mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value-preserving and norm-preserving warps.
Is well-positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis, particularly as applications of these methods grow.
Motivates the proposed mixed effects models by referencing historical examples from 2D shape analysis, providing valuable context.
Includes effective strategies for implementing infinite-dimensional computations through projections onto basis functions, such as B-splines.
weaknesses: Comparisons are limited to a single state-of-the-art technique, 'warpMix', using its default R implementation parameters without attempting to match noise variances for a fairer comparison.

The default noise variance in 'warpMix' (around 10^-3) is significantly different from the proposed model's noise variances (sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1), raising concerns about the fairness of the comparison.

Fails to compare the proposed approach to other relevant literature, particularly papers by cited authors (e.g., Kurtek, Srivastava, and colleagues) that utilize similar norm-preserving action models.
Evaluation for real functional data examples relies heavily on subjective visual assessments of quality, rather than quantifiable metrics or real downstream tasks.
It is unclear if the estimated functions are useful in a quantifiable way, as there's no demonstration of improvement in downstream tasks like classification.
suggestions: Attempt to match the noise variance parameters between the 'warpMix' model and the proposed model for more equitable comparisons.
Include comparisons with other existing approaches from the literature that use similar norm-preserving action models, especially those by Kurtek, Srivastava, and colleagues.

Augment the evaluation of real functional data with objective, quantifiable metrics derived from downstream tasks (e.g., classification accuracy) to demonstrate the practical utility of the estimated functions.

Investigate and discuss the implications of implementing the method without basis function approximations (i.e., using raw samples), detailing what aspects would simplify or complicate, and the practical consequences of assuming specific families of basis functions like B-splines.
main_points: The paper introduces a novel Bayesian functional mixed model for fixed effect estimation in functional data, uniquely incorporating norm-preserving warping.
The clarity and depth in explaining norm-preserving warps and their practical implementation via basis functions are significant strengths.

Key criticisms include the limited and potentially unfair comparison with 'warpMix' due to mismatched noise variances, the omission of comparisons to other relevant literature, and the reliance on subjective visual assessments for real-data evaluation.
The reviewer also raises questions regarding the practical implications and performance of the method when varying the choice and approximation of basis functions.
technical_details: The model targets functional data, specifically time-series represented as functions.
It utilizes a Bayesian approach for estimating model parameters.
The observation model accounts for a fixed effect (mean function), noise functions (variations), and a norm-preserving warping function.
The paper assumes specific distributions for noise terms and employs basis functions (e.g., B-splines, modified Fourier basis) for finite-dimensional representation.
Comparisons were made against 'warpMix', a frequentist functional mixed model with a publicly available R implementation.
Concerns were raised about the comparability of noise variances: 'warpMix' default noise variance at ~10^-3 vs. proposed model's sigma_c^2 = 0.25 and sigma^2 = 1.
MCMC sampling using Metropolis-Hastings algorithm is employed for posterior inference.

Two prior models for phase functions (gamma_i) are used: PM1 (a one-parameter family, gamma_i(t) = t + alpha_i*t*(t-1)) and PM2 (a nonparametric finite-dimensional family based on a Dirichlet process, p(gamma_i) ind~ Dirichlet(theta_gamma*t)).
Data-driven functional PCA basis was explored in the appendix and performed worse than B-splines for modeling the fixed effect function.

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.

Strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).

## Novelty:

Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Overall Recommendation: Accept with Minor Revisions (Rating: 7)**

This paper presents a compelling Bayesian functional mixed model designed to robustly recover the size-and-shape of a fixed effect function µ, specifically addressing challenges posed by both measurement errors and object-level phase variations. The model's formulation, which adeptly focuses on size-and-shape invariant characteristics, is well-supported by numerical experiments conducted on both synthetic and real datasets. The work is a valuable contribution to the field, offering a principled approach to a complex problem.

### Strengths

The manuscript demonstrates several notable strengths. Firstly, the overall presentation is remarkably clear, self-contained, and precise, making the complex methodology accessible to readers. Secondly, the model's formulation is meticulously developed, with careful justifications provided for the choice of various components, such as the basis functions and prior distributions. The theoretical underpinnings are effectively complemented by a comprehensive set of numerical experiments that validate the model's performance.

### Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement

While the paper is strong, several aspects could be further clarified or elaborated to enhance its impact and rigor.

1.  **Significance of the Phase-Preserving Model:** The core significance and real-world applicability of the proposed phase-preserving model (equations 3 and 4) are not adequately justified. It is unclear why this specific formulation is chosen, its reasonableness for practical applications, and its conceptual benefits or drawbacks when compared to alternative value-preserving actions.
    *   **Suggestion:** Please provide a more in-depth discussion on the significance of the phase-preserving model, including its applicability to real-world problems and a conceptual comparison of its benefits and drawbacks against value-preserving actions.

2.  **Theoretical Rigor of Norm-Preserving Action:** The explanation regarding why the norm-preserving action leads to a "larger" equivalence class of functions (lines 142-147 and footnote 1) is currently insufficient. It lacks a rigorous proof or reference to substantiate this claim.
    *   **Suggestion:** A more rigorous explanation or formal proof for the claim that the norm-preserving action yields a "larger" equivalence class of functions is required to strengthen this theoretical point.

3.  **Empirical Validation on Real Data:** The comparison with `warpMix` on real data (Figure 4) is limited by the absence of ground truth. This makes it challenging to objectively assess which method is truly superior, thus limiting the empirical validation of the method's advantages.
    *   **Suggestion:** While ground truth may be unavailable, further discussion on the implications of this limitation and perhaps qualitative observations that highlight the proposed method's strengths would be beneficial.

4.  **Optimality of Modeling Choices and Ambiguity Resolution:** The paper could benefit from a discussion on the theoretical implications of its modeling choices for resolving parameter ambiguity. Specifically, the optimality of enforcing constraints (e.g., near line 76) to resolve ambiguity should be explored, potentially referencing related work (e.g., Hartley 1997, Ding et al. 2020) and considering alternative strategies.
    *   **Suggestion:** Please discuss the optimality of the model's choices for resolving ambiguity and explore potential alternative approaches or the theoretical basis for the chosen constraints.

5.  **Clarity and Narrative Flow:**
    *   **Introduction:** The introduction is somewhat confusing, as it introduces multiple problems that tend to bury the key issue of phase variability, which is central to the paper's contribution.
        *   **Suggestion:** Restructure the introduction to more clearly highlight the central issue of phase variability and its importance as the primary problem addressed by the paper.
    *   **Section 2 Preamble:** Section 2, which discusses phase functions and transformations, lacks a clear preamble explaining the importance of these properties to the solution being proposed.
        *   **Suggestion:** Add a preamble to Section 2 to clarify why the discussed phase function properties are important and how they contribute to the overall solution.

6.  **Parameter Estimation Methods:** The paper utilizes MCMC for parameter estimation. A brief comment on the comparison between using MCMC for posterior sampling and optimization-based methods (e.g., maximizing the log-likelihood) would be valuable for context.
    *   **Suggestion:** Comment on the comparison between using MCMC for parameter estimation and optimization-based approaches (e.g., maximizing the log-likelihood).

### Minor Technical Point

*   Please check the notation "gamma in Gamma" (line 119) for potential typos or clarity.

### Conclusion

This is a well-written and significant paper that proposes an innovative Bayesian functional mixed model. The strengths in its clear presentation, careful formulation, and numerical validation are substantial. Addressing the identified weaknesses, particularly regarding the theoretical justifications for the phase-preserving model and the norm-preserving action, as well as refining the narrative flow, will significantly strengthen the manuscript. These suggested revisions are primarily clarifications and elaborations that will enhance the paper's overall impact and rigor. I recommend acceptance with minor revisions.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.

Strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

Summary:
The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.

Strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.

Weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.

Questions:
I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.

Limitations:
All limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper introduces an advanced additive model designed for uncertainty quantification in one-dimensional regression tasks with noisy observations. The proposed method leverages invariances under space-time unitary transformations and employs Bayesian inference within a functional mixed model framework. Numerical experiments, including a comparison against classical methods like Gaussian Processes (GPs), suggest its superiority in specific scenarios.

**Overall Assessment:**

While the paper presents a technically sophisticated approach that demonstrates strong performance in its niche, I have significant reservations regarding its suitability and accessibility for the NeurIPS audience. The advanced mathematical content and the highly specialized problem domain make it challenging for a broader machine learning community to fully appreciate or engage with the work. Consequently, my recommendation is to **reject** the paper at this venue.

**Strengths:**

*   **Robust Performance in Specific Scenarios:** The method shows promise in recovering the true underlying signal on synthetic datasets, even under high-noise conditions where classical approaches like Gaussian Processes may struggle. This robustness appears to stem from the strong modeling assumptions made.
*   **Demonstrated Utility:** The application of the method to the Berkeley growth spurt dataset provides a concrete example of its potential utility within its specific domain.
*   **Addressed Limitations:** The authors have adequately addressed the limitations they themselves raised within the paper, indicating a thoughtful self-assessment of their work.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Niche Focus and Limited Broad Appeal:** A primary concern is whether the work will genuinely interest a wider machine learning audience. The considered topic and the specific datasets feel quite niche, making the broader relevance of functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference unclear for many.
*   **High Barrier to Understanding:** Despite a well-written introduction, the paper quickly becomes very difficult to understand due to the advanced mathematical formalism and notation. This significant barrier to accessibility prevents a reviewer, and likely many readers, from fully grasping the nuances and appreciating the technical contributions without a substantial background in this specialized area of statistics.
*   **Unclear General Motivations:** The motivations and potential applications of the proposed method to more general machine learning contexts are not sufficiently clarified. This exacerbates the feeling that the work might be too specialized for a general-purpose ML conference.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

*   **Clarify Broader Motivations:** For future submissions to a machine learning venue, the authors should strive to clarify the more general motivations for their work. This would involve drawing clearer connections to common machine learning problems or demonstrating how the underlying principles could extend beyond the current niche applications.
*   **Consider Alternative Venues:** Given the paper's strong focus on advanced statistical modeling, the frequent referencing of statistics journals throughout the text, and the highly specialized nature of the content, the paper might be better suited for a specialized statistics journal or a conference with a more focused statistical methodology track. This could allow the work to receive the appreciation it deserves from an audience better equipped to engage with its technical depth.

**Conclusion:**

While the paper presents a technically sound and potentially valuable contribution to a specialized field, the significant challenges in accessibility and the unclear relevance to a broad machine learning audience make it a difficult fit for NeurIPS. The advanced mathematical content and the niche application domain are likely to limit its impact within this particular venue.

reviews.summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.
reviews.strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.
reviews.questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.

Strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.

Questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
This paper addresses the challenging and important task of reliably recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown fixed effect function µ by sampling and summarizing its posterior distribution within a functional mixed model framework. The model is notable for its ability to incorporate random object-level phase and amplitude components without making the often restrictive assumption of a finite-rank covariance for the error process. The core contribution lies in providing a robust Bayesian functional mixed model for the reliable recovery and uncertainty quantification of µ's size-and-shape.

**Strengths:**

The authors are commended for tackling such a challenging problem, which has significant implications for various fields. A key strength of the manuscript is its interesting theoretical analysis, particularly the detailed derivation of the Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio, which underpins the proposed sampling methodology. Furthermore, the paper includes a convergence analysis that is well-supported by numerical experiments, effectively demonstrating the utility and stability of the model.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**

While the theoretical framework is robust and well-presented, there are several areas where the manuscript could be strengthened to enhance its practical applicability and clarity.

1.  **Prior Models:** The paper does not extensively discuss the need for or provision of different prior models tailored for various types of functions. Providing guidance or examples of how to specify different prior models for different functional forms would significantly enhance the method's practical utility.
2.  **Parameter Sensitivity Analysis:** A notable omission is the lack of a parameter sensitivity analysis for the parameter θ. Such an analysis is crucial for understanding the model's robustness to hyperparameter choices and would provide valuable insights for practitioners.
3.  **Computational Efficiency:** The running time of the proposed method is not reported. Including information on the computational cost would be essential for assessing the practical feasibility and scalability of the approach, especially when dealing with large datasets or complex functions.
4.  **Comparative Advantage:** The paper would benefit from a clearer articulation of the advantages of the proposed method compared to other established techniques for function recovery by sampling, particularly against modern approaches like neural networks. A discussion highlighting its unique strengths and niche would be valuable.
5.  **Arbitrary Function Recovery:** Further explanation is needed regarding how arbitrary functions can be recovered by the fixed model presented in Equation (3). Clarifying the model's generalizability and its capacity to represent a wide range of functions would improve its interpretability and appeal.

**Conclusion:**

Overall, this paper presents a valuable contribution to the field, offering a robust Bayesian functional mixed model for the recovery and uncertainty quantification of fixed effect functions. The theoretical framework is strong, and the numerical experiments demonstrate the model's potential. Addressing the aforementioned points, particularly regarding practical considerations, comparative analysis, and model interpretation, would significantly enhance the manuscript's impact and clarity. I recommend acceptance with minor revisions.

reviews.summary: In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.
reviews.strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.
reviews.questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
This manuscript introduces a novel Bayesian functional mixed model operating within a functional Hilbert space. A key innovation of the proposed framework is its utilization of an isometric action of an infinite-dimensional group of phase functions to reliably infer the size-and-shape of a fixed effect function. Through numerical experiments, including comparisons with the state-of-the-art `warpMix` method, the authors demonstrate the model's effectiveness in capturing the main properties of the function, often showing superior performance.

The paper presents several commendable strengths. Foremost is the development of a Bayesian functional mixed effects model that accommodates an unrestricted form of phase variation, offering a flexible and powerful approach. The concept of inferring a size-and-shape function, framed as a geometric constraint, represents a novel and insightful perspective in this domain. Furthermore, the manuscript is generally concise, direct, and easy to follow, with clear explanations and discussions provided throughout, making the core ideas accessible to the reader.

While the paper offers significant contributions, there are several areas where improvements could enhance clarity, rigor, and completeness.

Firstly, regarding notation, the current presentation could benefit from greater precision. It is suggested that the authors improve clarity by consistently distinguishing between vectors, matrices, and scalars, and by including dimensionality where helpful. Inconsistent use of symbols, such as the transpose operator, should also be addressed to ensure mathematical rigor.

A practical motivation for the study, explaining its broader importance and potential applications, is currently lacking. Including such a discussion would significantly strengthen the paper's impact and appeal.

A core aspect of the model involves assuming a priori known ranks for the subspaces of basis functions (Bf and Br), following an LDA-style formulation. The manuscript does not provide sufficient discussion on how these ranks are fixed, nor does it analyze the sensitivity of the solution to these crucial parameters. It would be highly beneficial for the authors to provide a detailed discussion on the determination of Bf and Br, assess the sensitivity of the solution, and explore methods for automatically inferring these ranks.

The choice of B-spline basis functions is mentioned but not thoroughly justified. The authors should either provide a robust justification for this specific choice or investigate the use of other piecewise polynomial functions, or even consider the possibility of learning both basis functions jointly with their weight coefficients.

The experimental design also presents several points for consideration. A large discrepancy is observed between the synthetic data size (n=100,000) and real data size (n=93) used in experiments. It is recommended that synthetic data scenarios also consider limited data sizes to better reflect real-world conditions. Additionally, Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction and should be referenced appropriately. The comparison with `warpMix`, while insightful, would be stronger if expanded to include a wider range of datasets. A crucial missing element is a comparative analysis of computational cost (accuracy vs. cost trade-off) for both the proposed method and `warpMix`. Finally, the paper would benefit from a discussion of potential failure cases of the model and a more thorough analysis of its overall limitations.

In summary, this paper introduces a novel Bayesian functional mixed model that reliably infers the size-and-shape of a fixed effect function by leveraging an isometric action of phase functions. The proposed model demonstrates superior performance over the `warpMix` method in the reported experiments and offers a fresh perspective on functional data analysis. While the reviewer has identified several areas for improvement, particularly concerning notation, justification of model parameters, and a more comprehensive experimental evaluation, these issues are addressable through revisions. The core contributions are significant and innovative. Therefore, despite the identified weaknesses, I see substantial merit in this work and recommend **acceptance**.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5

title: Chain of Preference Optimization: Improving Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.
reviews.limitations: Yes
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.

Strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.

Limitations:
Yes

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
**Overall Assessment:**

The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO), a novel method designed to enhance the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) by leveraging preference data generated during the Tree-of-Thought (ToT) process for fine-tuning. A significant advantage of CPO is its ability to improve performance without incurring additional inference complexity. The core idea is interesting, and the paper is well-written and easy to follow. While the approach holds considerable promise, several critical areas require further exploration and analysis to fully validate its scope and robustness. With appropriate revisions addressing the weaknesses outlined below, the paper would be a valuable contribution.

**Strengths:**

*   **Novelty of CPO:** The central idea of Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) is genuinely interesting, presenting a novel approach to improving LLM reasoning by utilizing ToT-generated preference data.
*   **Efficiency:** A key strength of this method is its capacity to achieve enhanced performance without increasing the inference complexity, which is a significant practical advantage for real-world applications.
*   **Clarity:** The paper is well-written and easy to follow, making the proposed methodology accessible and understandable to readers.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Limited Evaluation on Complex Benchmarks:** The paper currently lacks an evaluation of CPO's performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark. This limits the understanding of its applicability and effectiveness in highly challenging, multi-step problem-solving tasks.
*   **Potential Reduction in Path Diversity:** Fine-tuning with ToT-generated reasoning paths may inadvertently reduce the diversity of generated paths. This could potentially lead to models that are less flexible or robust in exploring alternative solutions when faced with varied or ambiguous problems.
*   **Dependency on ToT Path Quality:** The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the initial quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. The paper does not adequately discuss potential vulnerabilities or mitigation strategies if the initial ToT paths are suboptimal.
*   **Applicability to Advanced Models:** The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced and state-of-the-art LLM architectures, such as LLaMA3. This omission limits the perceived relevance and generalizability of the method in the current rapidly evolving LLM landscape.
*   **Lack of Interpretability Analysis:** The paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding the underlying preferences learned by CPO and why certain paths are favored is crucial for building trust in the model's reasoning process and for future improvements.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To significantly strengthen the paper and address the identified weaknesses, I recommend the following:

1.  **Investigate Path Diversity:** Conduct a systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning with CPO. This analysis would provide crucial insights into whether path diversity is indeed reduced and its implications.
2.  **Enhance Interpretability Analysis:** Perform a deeper analysis on the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. This could involve qualitative examples or quantitative metrics to understand the preferences CPO learns and how they contribute to improved reasoning.
3.  **Expand Evaluation to Complex Benchmarks:** Explore CPO's performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, to demonstrate its generalizability and robustness on challenging tasks.
4.  **Discuss Applicability to Advanced Models:** Address the applicability of CPO to more advanced and recent LLM architectures, such as LLaMA3, to showcase its potential impact on cutting-edge models and ensure its relevance in ongoing research.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.
reviews.strengths: The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. This indicates that CPO effectively fine-tunes LLMs to generate more optimal reasoning paths, thereby improving their performance in complex problem-solving tasks. The research is significant as it provides a practical solution to the challenge of balancing inference complexity and reasoning quality in LLMs.
reviews.weaknesses: The paper presents a promising approach to fine-tuning LLMs using CPO. However, there is a significant gap in the evaluation of the potential impacts of this fine-tuning process on other abilities of the pretrained LLMs. This omission could leave readers questioning the broader implications and versatility of the proposed method. Future work should consider a comprehensive evaluation of the fine-tuning process to fully understand its effects on the LLMs' overall performance.
